one_line_takeaway: A CVPR-workshop challenge report establishing a robustness benchmark for face-swap deepfake detection under adversarial and accidental degradations, where DINOv3-based foundation-model ensembles with degradation augmentation won (ShallowReal, 0.917 private AUC) and a private test set confirmed the top rankings were not overfit.
key_finding: The winning team ShallowReal reached 0.9218 AUC public / 0.9168 private (DINOv3-Large + LoRA r=32 + a Multi-Aspect Classification head aggregating CLS/register/AVG tokens, plus dynamic-resolution, deep supervision, supervised-contrastive loss, and stochastic depth). Rankings were stable between public and private test sets, so the organizers report no evidence of test-set overfitting among top teams. Notably, AntInternational posted the single highest public AUC (0.9234) but dropped to 0.8691 on the private set (rank 3), illustrating the value of the private-set control. Across the field the recurring recipe is large self-supervised foundation backbones (DINOv3 dominates the leaderboard; also CLIP/MetaCLIP2/SigLIP), model ensembling with global+local (patch/MIL) branches, and heavy PMM-style degradation augmentation. Organizers conclude high-quality pretraining prevents overfitting while degradation exposure during training buys robustness.
core_contribution: This is the organizers' report on the first NTIRE 2026 Robust Deepfake Detection Challenge. The organizers' own contribution is the challenge design and benchmark, not any single detector: they built a four-split face-forgery dataset (all derived from CelebV-HQ, all face-crop images) that deliberately couples fake-generator shift with common AND uncommon image degradations, and ran a competition with anti-overfitting controls (24h test window, single test submission, no test labels, and a held-out private test set on which the top teams' shipped code/weights were re-scored). The paper then aggregates and compares the 14 finally submitted team methods and draws the cross-cutting finding that large pretrained foundation-model backbones + ensembling + explicit degradation-augmentation training are what deliver both generality and robustness. The individual per-team method descriptions (Secs 3.1-3.14) are participant contributions reported by the organizers, not the organizers' own algorithmic novelty.
threat_model: Robustness of deepfake detectors to image degradation, framed explicitly as a security problem. Two threats: (1) accidental/benign quality loss in real-world image-processing pipelines (noise, JPEG, resize, color shifts) that silently collapses detector performance, and (2) an active adversary who deliberately introduces degradations to a fake image to exploit the detector's weakness and evade detection (the organizers cite their prior PMM work [24] showing degradations can be maliciously used to circumvent detection). The challenge also folds in cross-generator generalization (unseen fake methods FSGAN and others only appear in test/private splits) as an implicit evasion axis. The defender's goal is a detector whose AUC stays high on unknown, out-of-domain degradations and unseen generators.
limitations: Scope is narrow: images only (single face crops, no video/temporal modeling), only face-swap/reenactment fakes (fully-synthetic faces explicitly excluded), and all data derives from a single source distribution (CelebV-HQ), limiting real-world diversity. Provided train/val sets are tiny (1000/100) so results depend heavily on whichever external datasets each team chose, making method comparisons confounded by unequal training data rather than clean algorithm comparisons. The organizers themselves note they cannot fully rule out manual labeling of the small val/test sets. The report gives only a single aggregate AUC per team on public/private sets; there is no per-degradation-type or per-generator breakdown, so which specific degradations or unseen generators actually break detectors is not quantified. Only the top 7 teams have private-test scores; ranks 8-14 report public AUC only.

datasets_used: CelebV-HQ (base source for all four challenge splits)
Challenge train split: 1000 images (50/50), fakes from FaceSwap, PMM-style degradations
Challenge validation split: 100 images (50/50), adds StyleFeatureEditor fakes + speckle/Poisson noise
Challenge public test split: 1000 images (50/50), adds FSGAN fakes + salt-and-pepper/B&W/overlay degradations
Challenge private test split: 1000 images (50/50), adds one more fake method + two more uncommon degradations

External datasets used by various teams: FF++/FaceForensics++, Celeb-DF-v2/v3/++, DFDC, DFDCP, DF40, DDL, HydraFake, ScaleDF, DeeperForensics-1.0, HIDF, RedFace, DeepFakeDetection, FFIW, FaceShifter, UADFV, FakeAVCeleb, OpenMMSec (~32.5K imgs)
quantitative_results: 337 participants, 57 submissions to final leaderboard, 14 finally submitted methods
Rank 1 ShallowReal: 0.9218 public AUC / 0.9168 private AUC (DINOv3-Large + LoRA r=32,alpha=64 + MAC head)
Rank 2 INTSIG (LOGER): 0.8901 public / 0.8824 private (5-model global+local DINOv3/MetaCLIP2 ensemble, ~700K training imgs)
Rank 3 AntInternational: 0.9234 public (highest public) / 0.8691 private (DINOv3 CLS + AttnPool ensemble, 35/65 weighting)
Rank 4 HCMUS-Aqua: 0.8775 public / 0.8523 private (multi-stream DINOv2-Giant + frozen CLIP-Large, 377,343 frames across 14 datasets)
Rank 5 acvlab: 0.8461 public / 0.8007 private (Select-and-Detect, CLIP ViT-L/14 + GroupDRO expert routing)
Rank 6 Reagvis Lab: 0.8430 public / 0.7934 private (CLIP-GAPL + DINOv3-GenD degradation-aware prototype fusion)
Rank 7 Hit Virlab: 0.8418 public / 0.7620 private (FSFM ViT-B + HAFA + degraded-original consistency)
Ranks 8-14 (public AUC only): Anonymous 0.839, Zeke 0.824, TCD Vision 0.8045, PSU 0.7056, AI4Good 0.6865, Acube 0.6860, NTR 0.5999
HCMUS-Aqua recovery pipeline reduced spatial parsing failure rate from 15% to 1.8%
baselines_compared: PMM (Practical Manipulation Model, Hopf & Timofte 2025) — organizers' prior work, used as the degradation-model baseline for dataset construction and cited as motivating the malicious-degradation threat
FaceSwap — baseline fake-generation method for the training split
No single reference detector baseline; the 14 participant teams are compared against each other on shared public/private AUC
Foundation-model backbones evaluated by teams: DINOv3 (Large/Huge/ViT-B/16), DINOv2-Giant, CLIP ViT-L/14, MetaCLIP2-Huge, SigLIP, EVA-giant/EVA02, I-JEPA, ConvNeXt, EfficientNet-V2, DeiT, FSFM ViT-B
